Having the Observatory timeout issue on a specific iPhone 12 Pro, tried cleaning the Xcode cache, doing flutter clean, reinstalling the Flutter SDK and Xcode, clearing trusted devices on the iPhone, doing a hard reset on iPhone... IOS: 14.8 Flutter: 2.5.1 Xcode: 13.0 flutter doctor -v Error message: iOS Observatory not discovered after 30 seconds. This is taking much longer than expected… Error launching application on iPhone.
If anyone was having the Observatory timeout issue for a specific device and no other solutions worked, try resetting that device to the factory settings. It worked for me.

Also suffering from this issue. Just gonna share some solutions that I've found to work so far:

```
> **iOS Observatory not discovered after 30 seconds.**

**Replace file (~/flutter/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart):**
> final Timer timer = Timer(discoveryTimeout ?? const Duration(seconds: 30), () {

**To**
> final Timer timer = Timer(discoveryTimeout ?? const Duration(seconds: 60), () {

**Or**
> flutter pub cache repair
> cd ios && pod cache clean --all && pod deintegrate && pod install

**Or**
> Run with xcode first and retry.
```
I suspect at least some of these reports are duplicates of https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/42969.
I have same problem
Solved my problem by running build on XCode first (`Product` -> `build`) then run `flutter run`

**Following works for me:**

**1st issue:** 
 I upgraded iOS from 14 to 15, and apparently device support for iOS 15 was missing.
This [answer](https://stackoverflow.com/a/63232598/3241111) resolves the issue

**2nd issue:** 
Flutter project runs fine from Xcode, but from terminal or Android Studio, it sometimes crashes/runs with error "Observatory port". Some suggesting changing timeout from 30 to 200ms in `flutter/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart`  but didn't work, so reverted.
```
localUri = await observatoryDiscovery.uri.timeout(const Duration(seconds: 200));
```


**This isn't best but workable solution** https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/51269#issuecomment-851690757

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6055628/141694668-548b5b6a-d745-42ae-a178-d63290d2a209.png)
Run from Xcode, then run following command in terminal.

Instead of this commend
`flutter attach --debug-url <my-url>`, but looks like debug-url doesn't exist in Flutter v2.2.3.

this worked for me
`flutter attach --host-vmservice-port <my-port>`

-------

Tip 1: Try with new flutter project, so build time is less.


Then tried running on our actual project, it threw same Observatory error. Run with `--verbose` , it threw mDNS meta error which was resolved using https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/46705

@gutisalex, you're right: Just **starting Xcode with my iPhone connected** and letting it do its thing **fixed it for me**! 

**Details**
I had updated my iPhone to iOS 15.2, and my watch to watchOS 8.3. Now for each connected device and each OS version, Xcode is fetching debug symbols when they are connected for the first time:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1061209/146170061-849e91cf-04b1-42dc-9070-16126b961cc8.png)

So Xcode fetched debug symbols for both my iPhone and my watch. Once that was over, I closed Xcode. And then Flutter ran my app just fine! 😀

> 2 years old issue, happens every time... no response or fix...

@armandojimenez Just starting Xcode with my iPhone connected and letting it do its thing fixed it for me! Somehow, new iOS releases force Xcode to copy "debug information" from my iPhone and Apple Watch.

Reposting this yet again:

1. The flood of reports of this issue usually coincides with the iOS updates.  Long app launch times is a commonly reported problem when there's a pending iOS update on your test device, or when your test device is running a newer OS than the one shipped with Xcode.  It's also commonly reported right after you upgrade your test OS because Xcode can get stuck trying to copy the developer image over to the device.  Many of these reports are that it works on one device and not on another device running the same OS.  Frankly there's not much Flutter can do to work around this when the problem is between the test device and Xcode.
2. Make sure Xcode is updated and your iOS device is updated.  You can also try opening `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` in Xcode and running your app from there to unwedge it.
3. You can also try running `flutter clean` to clean Xcode's build folder (DerivedData).
4. It seems like a cop-out, but swapping your cable may also help.
5. People are also reporting uninstalling the app first may help.  We tried that in our own CI system and added an `--uninstall-first` flag to `run` and `drive`, but it didn't work for us, but maybe it will for you. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102948
6. You also may be hitting https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/42969.  See if there's a pop up under your windows.

We've seen the "busy fetching symbols" issue in Flutter's own CI system when trying to tether to physical phones https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/103511.  The only workaround we've come up after extensive trial and error is to open Xcode, wait awhile, then close Xcode https://github.com/flutter/cocoon/pull/1897.  It's really annoying since we're not in control of this--Xcode handles getting the debug symbols over to the device.  There's nothing for us to "fix" here, Xcode has a bug from their command line tooling and we're along for the ride.

One thing we could do is to detect the "busy fetching symbols" error and tell the user to try opening Xcode until the message goes away from Window > Devices and Simulators so at least users in this state have a way to "fix" it without getting stuck.

I'm going to lock this issue to keep the "me too" comments from pushing this out a third time.  Subscribe to the issue to get updates from Flutter contributors.

If you are seeing something NOT resolved by the above, please file a new issue so we can track down anything actionable that isn't an Xcode bug.

I am running into this issue with a real device every time. I have not updated XCode or iOS for a while. Nor is XCode showing any type of syncing of debugging symbols. I will note I'm using an iPhone 6s 64GB as a test device.

The iPhone 6s is from 2015, and the performance is not top notch. Single threaded performance is the most important for starting the observatory server in time. In particular, the A9 processor on the iPhone 6s has about 1/3 of the single threaded performance of the A15 processor in an iPhone 13. In my experience, after I manually increased the timeout, it takes around 70 seconds for the observatory service to come up from the moment of app launch on the device (I'm using https://github.com/gskinnerTeam/flutter-wonderous-app as a test).

Since Flutter can run on many generation of devices for compatibility reasons, it is best not to assume all devices will handle starting up the observatory service in 30 seconds. Currently, Flutter supports iOS 11+, and even older phones (iPhone 5s/6) on iOS 12 are still supported. An option to increase this timeout easily or making the timeout depend on device model could be considered.

Of course, for the vast majority of people who have new devices, point 1,2, and 4 in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/72161#issuecomment-1167965559 is good advice. That could possibly be added next to https://docs.flutter.dev/add-to-app/ios/project-setup#local-network-privacy-permissions to note that behavior.

Revisiting this issue to check if the originally reported behavior of observatory port timeout still occurs using latest stable version of the framework.



> The problem is below and the solution is to increase the timeout.
> 
> https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/44d0e52d2065dbcaa01ac29b4488c7bbd7b1e76c/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart#L430

I do not this this code implementation anymore in the said file. Rather see below:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/d2631b552c4a1544f34a2c0d0fa7ecb573114202/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart#L587

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/d2631b552c4a1544f34a2c0d0fa7ecb573114202/packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/devices.dart#L595

Also, as mentioned below are some of the takeaways from the issue:



> * The flood of reports of this issue usually coincides with the iOS updates.  Long app launch times is a commonly reported problem when there's a pending iOS update on your test device, or when your test device is running a newer OS than the one shipped with Xcode.  It's also commonly reported right after you upgrade your test OS because Xcode can get stuck trying to copy the developer image over to the device.  Many of these reports are that it works on one device and not on another device running the same OS.  Frankly there's not much Flutter can do to work around this when the problem is between the test device and Xcode.
> * Make sure Xcode is updated and your iOS device is updated.  You can also try opening `ios/Runner.xcworkspace` in Xcode and running your app from there to unwedge it.
> * You can also try running `flutter clean` to clean Xcode's build folder (DerivedData).
> * It seems like a cop-out, but swapping your cable may also help.
> * People are also reporting uninstalling the app first may help.  We tried that in our own CI system and added an `--uninstall-first` flag to `run` and `drive`, but it didn't work for us, but maybe it will for you. [[flutter_tools] add --uninstall-first flag and pipe it through to ios-deploy #102948](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/102948)
> * You also may be hitting ["iproxy cannot be opened because the developer cannot be verified" #42969](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/42969).  See if there's a pop up under your windows.

Moreover, we haven't seen this issue report using latest or recent stable version since a long time.

@stx or anyone from this thread who reported it earlier, is there a way for you to check and confirm if you are still getting or saw this timeout issue on recent or latest stable version ?

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the application
2. Confirm camera permissions
3. See improperly rotated preview

### Expected results

The camera preview should be properly rotated.

### Actual results

The camera preview is rotated 90º.

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:camera/camera.dart';
import 'package:flutter/foundation.dart';
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() async {
  runApp(const MainApp());
}

class MainApp extends StatefulWidget {
  const MainApp({super.key});

  @override
  State<MainApp> createState() => _MainAppState();
}

class _MainAppState extends State<MainApp> {
  final _runner = _Runner();

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(
        body: ValueListenableBuilder(
          valueListenable: _runner,
          builder: _build,
        ),
      ),
    );
  }

  Widget _build(BuildContext context, _State value, Widget? child) {
    switch (value) {
      case _Loaded():
        return Center(
          child: CameraPreview(value.controller),
        );

      case _Simple.initializing:
        return const Center(
          child: CircularProgressIndicator(),
        );

      case _Simple.error:
        return const Center(
          child: Icon(Icons.error),
        );
    }
  }
}

class _Runner with ChangeNotifier implements ValueListenable<_State> {
  _Runner() {
    _init();
  }

  void _init() async {
    final available = await availableCameras();
    if (available.isEmpty) {
      _emit(_Simple.error);
    } else {
      final controller =
          CameraController(available.first, ResolutionPreset.veryHigh);
      try {
        await controller.initialize();
        _emit(_Loaded(
          controller: controller,
          available: available,
        ));
      } catch (e) {
        _emit(_Simple.error);
      }
    }
  }

  _State _value = _Simple.initializing;
  @override
  _State get value => _value;
  void _emit(_State state) {
    _value = state;
    notifyListeners();
  }
}

sealed class _State {}

enum _Simple implements _State {
  initializing,
  error,
}

class _Loaded implements _State {
  final CameraController controller;
  final List<CameraDescription> available;

  const _Loaded({
    required this.controller,
    required this.available,
  });
}
```

</details>

**Additional:**
This issue reproduces on a Pixel 1 device. I wasn't able to test this on a more capable device.

The issue first occurs with the `camera_android_camerax: 0.6.8+2` if I override the dependency to: `camera_android_camerax: 0.6.7+2` everything works as expected.

An additional note, (while it is probably unrelated to this issue).

Currently, `camera_android`s `CameraPlatform.buildPreview` implementation uses a `Texture` directly, a single widget.
https://github.com/flutter/packages/blob/cc7b5fc79cd389bd02dba3dc7ee956761103b8b9/packages/camera/camera_android/lib/src/android_camera.dart#L536-L539

While `camera_android_camerax` does some additional preview shifting.
https://github.com/flutter/packages/blob/cc7b5fc79cd389bd02dba3dc7ee956761103b8b9/packages/camera/camera_android_camerax/lib/src/android_camera_camerax.dart#L856-L930

The concerning bit of potential mistakes is that the way to preview the camera is by using `package:camera/camera.dart#CameraPreview` which properly prepares the preview and further rotates it on Android platforms.

Are both plugin implementations aware of this? Or would it make more sense to have all plugins handle any potential rotation requirements on their own and the user would expect that `CameraPlatform.buildPreview` always returns the preview in the proper rotation without the need for additional manual corrections?

Thank you for a quick response.

As far as I can tell, the pull request to the issue you linked was made available in version `0.6.8+1`.
The issue reproduces for me on versions after `0.6.7+2`.

Please note, this issue is produced *after* the fix while running the regular skia engine.

The fix was meant for Impeller right? Which doesn't seem like the same issue.

I suppose the fix for Impeller caused a regression on Android devices running the regular skia engine.
I also tried running the app with impeller enabled. Same results.
Pixel 1 doesn't seem to support it.
@darshankawar Can you please reopen?
Thanks for feedback. Reopening for further triage.

@jellynoone Although we don't have Pixel 1 device, but we tried on Pixel 7 running Android 14 in which we didn't observe the rotated preview: This probably indicates it could be a device specific issue, but can you try on other devices (not pixel) to check if it persists still ?

I checked on an old Samsung (Android 5.1.1, API 22 (still a Flutter supported platform)) and everything worked as expected.

Regardless, even if this is a device specific issue, it is a regression that happened between `0.6.7+2` and `0.6.8+2`.

I will see if I can reproduce the issue on any other device, however, my options are currently limited.
